theory.intro: The highest common factor (HCF) of two algebraic terms is the largest term that divides into both exactly. Find it by combining the HCF of the numbers with the variables common to both terms.
facts: Find the **HCF of the coefficients** (the numbers in front) first.
A variable belongs in the HCF only if it appears in **both** terms.
For a shared variable raised to powers, take the **lower power** (e.g. $x^2$ and $x^5$ share $x^2$).
Multiply the numerical HCF by every common variable part to get the HCF.
If the coefficients share no factor, their HCF is $1$, but common variables are still kept.

foundation.solution_text:
$x^2 \text{ and } x^5$
$\text{take the lower power}$
$= x^2$

$2xy \text{ and } 8xy$
$\text{HCF of } 2, 8 = 2$
$x \text{ and } y \text{ in both}$
$= 2xy$

development.solution_text:
$8x^2y \text{ and } 12x^5y$
$\text{HCF of } 8, 12 = 4$
$x^2 \text{ is the lower power}$
$y \text{ in both}$
$= 4x^2y$

$12qr \text{ and } 8r^2$
$\text{HCF of } 12, 8 = 4$
$q \text{ is not in both}$
$r \text{ has lower power } r^1$
$= 4r$

$6xy \text{ and } 12x$
$\text{HCF of } 6, 12 = 6$
$x \text{ in both, } y \text{ is not}$
$= 6x$

mastery.solution_text:
$18a^3b^2c \text{ and } 24a^2b^4$
$\text{HCF of } 18, 24 = 6$
$a^2 \text{ and } b^2 \text{ are lower powers}$
$c \text{ is not in both}$
$= 6a^2b^2$

$15x^2y \text{ and } 8x^3$
$\text{HCF of } 15, 8 = 1$
$x^2 \text{ is the lower power}$
$y \text{ is not in both}$
$= x^2$

$20p^2q^3 \text{ and } 30p^4q$
$\text{HCF of } 20, 30 = 10$
$p^2 \text{ and } q \text{ are lower powers}$
$= 10p^2q$
